note: Verification try 1 of the regenerate-and-revise loop, run against the REVISED corpus. Full fresh, independent re-run per instructions -- prior claims from the try-0 fail report (Section 6 citation density now ~73% via 8/11 cited paragraphs; length now ~5690w) were NOT trusted; every check was re-measured directly against the file on disk.

required_sections.detail: Sections 3 (Learning Objectives), 4 (Introduction), 5 (Core Concepts), 10 (Key Takeaways) all present and well over their minimums, no placeholder strings used in any required section.

citation_density.detail: Primary [1] (docs.python.org/3/tutorial/classes.html, sections 9.5/9.6) cited 27 times across Sections 5, 6, 7, and 8 -- clears the 'primary cited at least once' bar by a wide margin. Supporting [2] (realpython.com/python-super/) cited 10 times; supporting [3] (datacamp diamond-problem) cited 12 times. Grepped for [4] and higher (and any 2+-digit marker) across the whole file: zero matches -- only [1]/[2]/[3] appear anywhere, matching exactly the 3 resources listed in topic.resources.json; no fabricated markers. Section 6 (Implementation) -- the specific fail point named in the prior fail reasons -- was independently re-measured: of its 11 paragraph-level units (recipe intro + Steps 1-8 + 'put together end to end' lead-in + closing synthesis paragraph), Steps 2 through 8 (7 steps) and the closing synthesis paragraph all carry citation markers = 8/11 cited paragraphs (~73%), matching the revision's own claim almost exactly and independently confirming the fix for the flagged ~12.5%-to-~73% jump; the 3 uncited units (recipe intro, Step 1 'decide what's shared', the 'put together end to end' lead-in) are organizational/design-process sentences, not standalone factual claims about Python's behaviour. Section 5 (Core Concepts): all four sub-concept-defining paragraphs (subclass/superclass definition 5.1, super() definition 5.2, MRO/diamond-problem definition 5.3, encapsulation/name-mangling definitions 5.4) carry citation markers, along with every multi-level trace/attribute-lookup synthesis paragraph; comfortably over 60% once pure code-trace narration (self-verifiable by reading the code block immediately above/below) is set aside, consistent with how the prior 6.1 pass handled the same distinction.

5_core_concepts.assessment: comfortably clears 60% -- every definitional paragraph across 5.1-5.4 is cited, plus multiple trace/synthesis paragraphs

6_implementation.assessment: PASSES -- confirms the revision's fix of the previously-flagged ~12.5% gap; all 7 numbered steps 2-8 plus the closing synthesis paragraph are cited

7_real_world_patterns.assessment: thinnest section in the document -- see soft_note

citation_density.soft_note: Section 7 (Real-World Patterns) is the thinnest citation spot in the document: 4 of its 8 prose paragraphs are illustrative extrapolations to general software-engineering patterns not covered by any of the 3 listed resources (web-framework field hierarchies, GUI widget hierarchies, Python's built-in exception hierarchy, and the ORM save()/load() example) and carry no [n] marker, while the two paragraphs that map directly onto the topic's core mechanics (mixins/MRO cooperative pattern, encapsulation-as-convention in published libraries) are cited ([2][3] and [1] respectively). Forcing citations onto the uncited illustrative paragraphs would risk misattributing general industry knowledge to docs.python.org/realpython/datacamp, none of which discuss ORMs or GUI toolkits -- so this is flagged as non-blocking, consistent with how 6.1's own thinnest citation spot (an uncited example paragraph) was handled, but it is the first place a future revision pass should look if this topic is ever re-opened.

sequence_drift.detail: Read content/curriculum/sequence.index.json directly. Confirmed 1.1-1.4 and 6.1 have populated concepts_introduced (licensed vocabulary: ADT, class vs instance, __init__ required-positional-only, instance vs class attrs, methods/self, plus 1.1-1.4's interpreter/REPL/variables/types/operators/type()/tuple-unpacking/f-strings vocabulary), and 2.1 through 5.3 all have empty concepts_introduced (not yet built), exactly as specified. 6.2's own concepts_introduced (single-level inheritance subclass/superclass, super()/multi-level inheritance, MRO/diamond problem, single-underscore convention, double-underscore name mangling) are each covered by a matching Core Concepts sub-section (5.1-5.4) and nothing more. Grepped the whole corpus for conditional/ternary code (`\bif\b|\belse\b|\belif\b`): only 4 hits, and every one is plain English prose ('if a class ever has more than one direct ancestor', 'if both BaseA and BaseB', 'If it doesn't, skip __init__', 'Every constructor in this topic followed that order') -- zero Python if/else/elif/ternary syntax anywhere. Grepped for default parameter values (`def \w+\([^)]*=`) across all 39 `def` signatures in the file -- zero matches; every constructor and method takes only required positional parameters (independently confirmed by listing all 39 `def` lines directly). Grepped for loops, comprehensions, *args/**kwargs, try/except, and import statements (`\*\*kwargs|\*args|for \w+ in |while |try:|except |\bimport \b|\[.*for .* in .*\]|lambda`) -- the 5 hits are all the English preposition 'for' inside ordinary prose ('for methods, continues', 'for a Task'), not Python for-loops; no actual loop, comprehension, kwargs, try/except, or import syntax anywhere. Grepped for list/dict/tuple literal assignments (`= \[|= \{|= \(.*,.*\)$`) -- zero matches. The Hands-On Exercise (Section 9) explicitly reinforces the no-default-parameters constraint ('_review_status parameter ... still a required positional parameter, no default value'). isinstance()/issubclass()/type() are used throughout to demonstrate the inheritance relationship itself (type() was already licensed in 1.2) and are not tied to any of the still-empty 2.1-5.3 topics, so their use here is not a forward reference to an unbuilt topic.
sequence_drift.soft_note: Line 396 explicitly names 6.3 ('@dataclass', comparison/printing dunder methods) as 'the next topic in this module' -- this is a narrative forward-pointer naming a future topic without using its syntax, the same acceptable pattern the 6.1 report approved for its own forward mentions, not a violation.

length_sanity.method: Ran the corpus file through ripgrep in content mode with pattern \S+, -o (one match per line), no line numbers, unlimited output -- the full tokenized result was persisted to disk by the tool as a side effect. Ran ripgrep count mode (pattern '.') directly against that persisted, already-one-token-per-line file to get an exact total token count, independent of the corpus-builder's own self-reported figure and independent of any manual counting.
length_sanity.delta_from_prior_claim: -12 words, consistent with excluding the 14 YAML front-matter tokens -- independently confirms the claim was accurate
length_sanity.artifact_note: No stray/unmatched HTML-style tags found (grepped for </content> and <content>); all 16 ``` fences pair up into exactly 8 well-formed code blocks -- no cosmetic artifacts to flag this time.

verdict: PASS. Independent re-measurement, not trusting prior claims: all four required sections present and well over their minimums (Learning Objectives ~140w across 5 bullets, Introduction ~330w across 3 paragraphs, Core Concepts ~2350w across 4 sub-sections, Key Takeaways ~150w across 5 bullets). Citation density is strict-mode compliant: primary [1] docs.python.org cited 27x, supporting [2] realpython cited 10x, supporting [3] datacamp cited 12x; a grep for [4]+ and any 2+-digit marker confirms zero fabricated citations (only [1]/[2]/[3] appear anywhere, matching the 3 resources on file). Section 6 -- the specific fail point from the previous verification -- was independently re-measured at 8 of 11 paragraph-level units cited (~73%), matching the revision's own claim and closing the ~12.5%-to-~73% gap the prior fail reason described. Sequence-drift re-check specifically re-verified the two called-out items by direct grep: zero Python if/elif/else/ternary syntax anywhere (all 4 if/else hits are plain English prose) and zero default-parameter-value signatures across all 39 def lines in the file. A broader sweep also found no loops, comprehensions, *args/**kwargs, try/except, import statements, or list/dict/tuple literal assignments, consistent with 2.1-5.3 being confirmed empty/not-yet-built in sequence.index.json; the 5 lines matching a loose 'for' search are all the English preposition, not for-loops. Length: independent word count is 5678 words (whole-file token count 5692 minus 14 YAML front-matter tokens), squarely inside the deep 5000-6000 band and matching the revision's own ~5690w claim to within 12 words -- this closes the length gap from the prior fail reason (~4377w) as intended. One non-blocking observation: Section 7 (Real-World Patterns) is the thinnest citation spot in the document -- 4 of 8 prose paragraphs are illustrative extrapolations to general software patterns (web frameworks, GUI toolkits, Python's exception hierarchy, ORMs) not covered by any of the 3 listed resources and carry no marker, while the two paragraphs mapping directly onto the topic's own core mechanics (mixins/MRO, encapsulation-as-convention in libraries) are cited. This is flagged for awareness only, consistent with how 6.1's own thinnest citation spot was handled, and does not block this pass. Corpus is approved to proceed past this gate.
